context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: State Intervention and the East Asian Miracle

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the neoliberal vision of the State? How does that differ from the role of the State in East Asia? What is the role of civil society or labor or democracy in the East Asian experience? What role, if any, does State intervention have in economic development theory? Consider this in the context of new institutionalist theories in the next class.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about neoliberal views of the state versus the East Asian model. I’m a little confused about how to compare state intervention, civil society/labor/democracy, and then connect that to new institutionalist theory. Could you help me figure out a clear structure first?
exchanges.tutor: Before we think about structure, let's make sure the ideas are clear. What do you already know or think about the neoliberal view of the state? What role does it say the state should play in an economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help with structure before engaging with the content. My role is to guide them toward their own thinking, not to provide an outline or structure for them. I should redirect them to the content first — once they understand the ideas, structure will follow naturally. I'll ask a question that gets them thinking about the substance, which is the foundation of any good structure.

exchanges.student: I think neoliberalism is basically pro-market and wants the state to stay limited, but I’m not fully sure how to say that more precisely. My guess is the state mainly protects property rights, enforces contracts, and maybe keeps markets competitive, instead of directly steering industry.
exchanges.tutor: That's a good instinct. Now think about it from the other direction — what would neoliberalism say is problematic about a state that picks specific industries to support, protects domestic firms from foreign competition, or directs credit to certain sectors? Why would that be seen as a problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuitive grasp of the neoliberal state's role. I should affirm what's right without over-praising, and push them to think more precisely — specifically about what neoliberalism says the state should NOT do, which will set up the contrast with East Asia naturally.

exchanges.student: I think neoliberalism would say that kind of state is distorting the market and making inefficient choices. Like, officials might back the wrong industries, create corruption, or keep weak firms alive just because of politics instead of actual performance.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — that's the neoliberal critique in a nutshell. Now here's the tension: countries like South Korea, Taiwan, and Japan did precisely those things — state-directed credit, protected industries, picking winners — and yet grew incredibly fast. What does that do to the neoliberal argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building good momentum and has correctly identified key neoliberal critiques (market distortion, inefficiency, rent-seeking/corruption). Now I should pivot them toward the East Asian case, which directly challenges these predictions — this is the heart of the prompt's contrast.

exchanges.student: It seems like it weakens the neoliberal argument, or at least shows it’s too simple. I’m guessing the East Asian cases suggest state intervention can actually help development if the state is capable and strategic, but I’m not sure how to say that without sounding like “the state should always intervene.”
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction to make. So what do you think made East Asian state intervention work, when the neoliberal critique says it should have failed? What was different about those states compared to, say, a corrupt or captured state?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a sophisticated distinction — they're avoiding overgeneralization, which is good critical thinking. I should affirm this nuance and push them to think about what made East Asian state intervention different or effective, which will naturally lead toward the 'embedded autonomy' or 'developmental state' concepts without me naming them directly.

exchanges.student: I think the difference was that these states weren’t just intervening randomly — they had strong bureaucracies and could discipline firms. Maybe “embedded autonomy” fits here? Like the state was connected enough to business to know what was going on, but not totally captured by it.
exchanges.tutor: Good — embedded autonomy is the right concept and you've explained it well. Now the prompt also asks about civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience. How would you characterize those in countries like South Korea or Taiwan during their high-growth periods?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified embedded autonomy and articulated it well. This is a key concept in the assignment. I should affirm it and now move them toward the other part of the prompt they haven't addressed yet — civil society, labor, and democracy in the East Asian experience — which is a distinct and important thread.

exchanges.student: This is where I get a little stuck. I think during the main high-growth period, labor was often controlled or repressed and democracy was limited, so growth wasn’t really driven by strong democratic participation. Does that mean civil society had a weaker role, at least early on?
exchanges.tutor: Right — so what does that imply for anyone who argues that democracy or strong civil society is a prerequisite for development? Does the East Asian case support or challenge that view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified labor repression and limited democracy in early East Asian development, which is an important and honest observation. I should affirm this and push them to think about what this means for the broader argument — specifically, what does it complicate or challenge about development theory? This also sets up the tension with new institutionalist theory.

exchanges.student: It seems like East Asia challenges that view, at least if the claim is that democracy has to come first for development to happen. But I’m unsure if I should say democracy was unimportant, because that feels too extreme. Maybe it’s more that rapid industrialization happened under authoritarian or semi-authoritarian states, even if democracy mattered later for accountability and distribution?
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-calibrated distinction. Now — the prompt asks you to connect all of this to new institutionalist theories. What do you know or think new institutionalism says about the role of institutions in development? How might it relate to what you've just said about the East Asian state?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good nuance again — distinguishing between democracy as a prerequisite for growth versus its role in later accountability and redistribution. This is sophisticated thinking. Now I should move them toward the final part of the prompt: new institutionalist theory. We're about 7 exchanges in, so I need to make progress toward wrapping up the conceptual groundwork so the student can write.

exchanges.student: I think new institutionalism says institutions matter because they shape incentives and how states and markets actually work, but I’m fuzzy on the details. So maybe the link is that East Asia wasn’t just “more state” — it was specific institutions that made state intervention effective, like competent bureaucracy, discipline, and state-business coordination?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong connection — you've essentially just outlined the through-line of your entire paper. You have the neoliberal view, the East Asian contrast, the civil society/labor/democracy tension, and the new institutionalist link. At this point, I'd encourage you to stop planning and start writing a draft. What's your opening argument going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent connection between the East Asian developmental state and new institutionalism — that it's not about quantity of intervention but quality of institutions. This is the core insight the prompt is pushing toward. We're at exchange 8, so I should affirm this, point out they now have all the building blocks, and push them toward writing rather than continuing to discuss concepts.

exchanges.student: I think my opening argument is that the neoliberal view sees the state mainly as a market-supporting referee, while the East Asian experience shows that states can also be active developmental actors. I just want help making that claim a little sharper so it also includes the point that successful intervention depended on institutions, and that growth didn’t necessarily begin with liberal democracy.
exchanges.tutor: That's your job to write, not mine — and you actually have all the ingredients already. Try drafting that opening sentence or two yourself, then share it here and we can discuss whether it captures what you intend.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help sharpen their language, which would mean I'm doing the writing for them — that violates the rules. I need to firmly redirect them to do their own writing while affirming the substance of what they have. I should remind them of my role and push them to attempt the language themselves.

exchanges.student: Okay, let me try: Neoliberal theory presents the state as a limited guarantor of markets, emphasizing property rights, contract enforcement, and macroeconomic stability over direct intervention. However, the East Asian experience suggests that under certain institutional conditions, states can successfully guide industrial transformation, even in contexts where labor was constrained and democracy was limited.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid opening — it's clear, accurate, and sets up the key tensions well. The phrase 'certain institutional conditions' is doing a lot of work though; make sure your paper actually unpacks what those conditions are. Now keep going — you have the conceptual foundation, so write the rest and come back if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a strong opening that captures the core tension accurately. I should give honest, direct feedback on its merits without grading it, and push them to now move forward with the rest of the paper rather than dwelling on the opening. The conversation has reached 10 exchanges, so I should encourage them to keep writing independently.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently drawing ideas out of the student rather than supplying them. No near-submission-ready content was provided; when the student asked for help sharpening their opening claim, the tutor explicitly declined and redirected the student to write it themselves. Scaffolding was well-sequenced, building from neoliberal basics to East Asian contrast to civil society to new institutionalism. Responses were concise and well-targeted, tone was supportive without being effusive, and the tutor gave useful process-level feedback (e.g., noting that 'certain institutional conditions' needed unpacking). No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never supplied final answers, thesis language, or submission-ready reasoning; it consistently posed questions that required the student to generate the key ideas. The one moment of potential concern (Turn 10 feedback) was brief, accurate, and pointed back to the student's own writing task rather than completing it. 1.2: Each turn built directly on the student's prior response, and the tutor diagnosed confusion (e.g., Turn 6 on civil society) before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process feedback, notably flagging that 'certain institutional conditions' was vague and needed unpacking in the paper. 2.1–2.2: The conversation progressed linearly without repetition or drift. 3.1–3.2: Responses were appropriately brief and interactive; tone was consistently encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
